data.title: Alibaba: Why It's My Preferred Bet Amid The U.S.-China Trade War (Upgrade)
data.description:
Is it a good time to be an Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) (OTCPK:BBAAY) investor right now, even as the trade war between the US and China has reached feverish new heights that could threaten to freeze the convoluted supply chain, and throwing the global economic order into further chaos?

While The Trump Administration has clearly recognized the need to implement his "Blink Wednesday" moment, or euphemistically known as the “Trump Put,” it brought some much-needed respite to investors who were preparing for worse turbulence, as the S&P 500 was tethering even closer into a debilitating bear market.

Thanks to the less hawkish Trump administration officials, the market was spared the moment of reckoning at least for the next 90 days. There is no certainty at this point in time about any extensions, as the US maintains that it will "negotiate in good faith" with its trading partners.

Yet, the ambiguities affecting the supply chain between the US and China, as well as how trade diversions could reroute the global economy may not have been contemplated yet even as analysts have been writing down their estimates for the US market.

Despite that, economists have momentarily lifted their optimism for the US economy to possibly escape a recession this year. However, with trade developments proceeding at an accelerated pace while contemplating the vicissitudes of the Trump administration, I believe everything remains on the line right now and investors must be prepared for more downside if the trade (including further trade conflicts) if negotiations do not emerge with positive outcomes that the market has anticipated.

For Alibaba, it might seem contradictory that I indicated that investors may not bear the full brunt of the ongoing trade war with the US. As a reminder, I maintained my cautious stance on Alibaba’s thesis in my previous update, but I wasn’t bearish. Yet, I also enunciated why the stock looks well-primed to continue its recovery, but I didn’t anticipate that the subsequent risk-off move to be attributed to Trump’s trade conflict with China so soon.

Previously, the Chinese economy was already hobbled by the policy adjustments that President Xi Jinping instituted several years back. As a result, they arguably crashed the property market, while also throttling consumer spending, and battering consumer confidence further.

Hence, it has even led to a threatening deflationary spiral that China has attempted to weave itself out of. As a result, companies such as Shein, Temu (PDD), and TikTok (BDNCE) marveled as they demonstrated more resilience given their global distribution model into larger consumer markets such as the US and EU. Well, that narrative worked really well, until President Donald Trump returned to the White House.

We all know what happened since then:

  • Import tariffs on China exports have reached a 145% high watermark, suggesting that trade flows between the two countries face a quick and imminent freeze, compelling businesses in cross-border trade to dig deep into their survival instincts and look for ways to mitigate the economic damage, which could drag on further.

  • Trade negotiations between the US and other trading partners have accelerated. Yet, given how Trump has clearly prioritized these deals ahead of the negotiation with China, we might now have clarity on US-China talks until most of these deals are concluded. And whether they can be completed within the 90-day mark is a big question mark. Yet, a possible extension cannot be ruled out, but that might mean any trade deals with China will likely take even longer and being further delayed.

  • China factories/exporters are already looking to cut down on their capacity and manpower, while seeking diversion opportunities into other markets in the meantime, as trade flows with the US no longer seem viable.

I believe these developments have substantive positive impact that could boost Alibaba in the near and medium term. Why?

  • Alibaba was previously reportedly slow to adapt more quickly to international commerce, as its China commerce segment accounts for the large majority of its sales and also underpins the company’s core profitability. In other words, Alibaba depends primarily on the Chinese consumer to sustain the profitability of its e-commerce operations. It’s such as profitable setup as compared to its loss-making international ventures, even though that segment is expected to trend toward being accretive to the company’s bottom-line. Yet, with a minimal exposure to the US market, Alibaba is expected to outperform PDD for some time to come if the US-China trade war worsens.

  • Temu has been making gains in the EU, although the possibility of the closing of the de minimis loophole might also rankle the company’s performance this year, if the EU decides to close the same "loophole" to mitigate the risks of possible dumping by “desperate” Chinese exporters who lost access to their biggest consumer market [USA]. Hence, Alibaba might be given a respite, as it seeks to leverage on its solid profitability through its China core commerce engine to pursue a market share expansion plan to capitalize on the chink in PDD's armor as it faces new risks in its fast fashion approach, as compared to Alibaba's more diversified e-commerce gameplan.

Next, we all know that Alibaba Cloud has been making resilient gains in the local cloud computing market, as the company has also committed to investing more than $50B over several years to capitalize on its AI growth initiatives. Since Alibaba’s customers are primarily domestic, it doesn’t really have to be concerned with the ongoing US-China trade war.

Moreover, I believe the company has demonstrated its AI chops with Apple (AAPL) lining up in partnership with BABA to launch Apple Intelligence in the world’s second-largest economy, as the Cupertino company seeks to redress the grievances of its botched AI strategy, while placating Chinese regulators with its Alibaba partnership.

Hence, I believe the growth prospects on the Alibaba-Apple branding is expected to move beyond just goodwill, but also see further monetization amid more confident enterprise spending as it also seeks to broaden its scope with state-owned enterprises.

It should help anchor Alibaba Cloud as a leader not just in developing the most cutting-edge AI models, but also scale up faster to improve its profitability ahead of its smaller peers. And let’s not forget that Alibaba has also charted a new expansion path outside of China, as it seeks out international users who may want to diversify their reliance on the US, probably seeing an opportunity for companies worried about the economic risks of being entangled too tightly with the US cloud computing companies, given the impending AI diffusions rule that may hamper their deployment of high-end AI chips or cloud computing opportunities outside of the US.

Hence, I believe we are already seeing the ripple effects from Chinese tech giants anticipating such opportunities to possibly gain share at the expense of US companies, something that the US tech leaders have already cautioned the market previously, if the US turns increasingly more restrictive on chips deployment even in allied nations.

Therefore, even Wall Street analysts mostly concur about the optimism in Alibaba’s earnings outlook, intimating these tariffs impact are not expected to be substantial to BABA’s near-term estimates. Hence, I believe it should undergird the bullish reversal assessed in its price action, demonstrating that the worst is very likely over, notwithstanding the recent pullback.

As a reminder, I highlighted that the previous rally took things too far as sharp surges often suggest investors turned FOMO too quickly, while ignoring potential downside risks that may not have been obvious back then. Hence, the derating imputed to the worsened US-China trade war has obviously afflicted market sentiments recently, underscoring the need to be patient, and not chase such spurts, to potentially improve risk/reward.

With the stock down more than 35% from its March 2025 highs through this week’s lows, I assess a consolidation above the 50-week moving average (blue line) seems increasingly likely, as dip buyers look for an opportunity to get more engaged with BABA.

And BABA is valued at a forward PEG ratio of just 0.6 (more than 50% below its peers). Hence, I’m confident that Alibaba’s tenacity, relatively low exposure to the US-China trade war and its growing cloud computing business should lend credence to a more bullish posture for the stock from here.

Still, it must be noted that Chinese consumers wouldn't be immune if the trade war drags on, although their massive surplus in savings might help to mitigate a disaster scenario, especially if nationalistic fervor could play a role to support the Chinese economy, as China views the US's action as unilateral, and highly unjustified.

Also, investors must also consider potential delisting risks that may reemerge if the trade war broadens into other areas of economic cooperations and partnerships, which could seriously impede the viability of the US listings. In that case, investors might want to consider direct listings in Hong Kong, although it may not entirely eliminate geopolitical risks, but it wouldn't face the delisting risks as the US ADRs might incur in the US.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ZCN: A Strategic Satellite Holding For Canadian Exposure
data.description:
ETF Overview

BMO S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index ETF (ZCN:CA) invests in a portfolio of about 222 Canadian stocks. The fund has a low expense ratio of 0.06%, better than the 0.18% expense ratio of the iShares S&P/TSX 60 Index ETF (XIU:CA). The fund has a higher exposure to energy and basic materials but lower exposure to the technology sector than the S&P 500 index. Therefore, the fund might be more sensitive to different phases of the economic cycles but will likely have an inferior performance prospect than the S&P 500 index. Hence, this fund is more suitable as a satellite holding rather than a core holding in one’s diversified portfolio.

Fund Analysis

ZCN’s portfolio construction methodology

Let us first look at how ZCN constructs its portfolio. The fund passively tracks the performance result of the S&P/TSX Capped Composite Index. The result is a portfolio that consists of large-cap, mid-cap, and small-cap Canadian equities. Since the index is market-cap weighted, the fund has a tilt towards large-cap stocks. As can be seen from the chart below, large-cap stocks represent about 77% of its portfolio whereas mid-cap and small-cap only comprise about 18% and 6% of its portfolio respectively.

Slightly higher smaller-cap stocks lead to slightly higher downside risk

Although ZCN’s portfolio has a natural tilt towards large-cap, its exposure to large-cap stocks is still lower than many other Canadian equity funds. For example, XIU, which tracks the top 60 Canadian stocks by market-cap has a much higher tilt towards large-cap. As can be seen from the table below, large-cap stocks account for 91% of XIU’s total portfolio. On the other hand, mid-cap and small-cap stocks account for only 9% and 0% of its portfolio.

ZCN

XIU

Large-Cap

77%

91%

Mid-Cap

18%

9%

Small-Cap

6%

0%

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 9, 2025), created by author

Smaller-cap stocks generally have weaker balance sheets and less established business models. As such, they tend to underperform in economic/market turmoil. Indeed, this has been the case in the past. As can be seen from the chart below, ZCN has typically declined slightly more than XIU in past major corrections, such as the corrections in 2016, end of 2018, 2020, and 2022.

ZCN has a higher exposure to energy and basic materials

Investors wishing to invest in funds that focus on Canadian stocks such as ZCN should be aware that the energy and basic materials sectors make up a significantly larger portion of ZCN’s portfolio compared to the S&P 500 index. As shown in the table below, these two sectors account for 28.7% of ZCN’s portfolio. In contrast, these two sectors comprise just 5.2% of the S&P 500 index. As we know, energy and materials are highly sensitive to economic and commodity cycles. While this can improve performance during rising demand and inflation, it also introduces higher downside risk during economic slowdowns than the S&P 500 index.

Source: Morningstar (accessed on April 9, 2025), created by author

ZCN has lower exposure to technology

ZCN currently has about 10.5% exposure to technology. This is low relative to the S&P 500 index’s exposure of 30.4%. As we know, the technology sector has been one of the fastest growing sectors in the past and is poised to deliver strong growth in the upcoming decade thanks to many technological megatrends (e.g. AI, cloud computing, IoTs, etc.) ZCN’s lower exposure to technology weakens the fund’s performance prospect. In other words, ZCN’s long-term performance return will likely be inferior to the S&P 500 index, which has a much higher exposure to technology. This has indeed been the case in the past. As can be seen from the chart below, ZCN’s total return of 98.8% in the past 10 years was much lower than the S&P 500 index's 186.4%.

ZCN’s concentration risk is moderate

As can be seen from the table below, ZCN’s top holding accounts for about 6.7% of its portfolio, higher than the S&P 500’s top holding, but lower than XIU’s top holding at 8.3%. Likewise, ZCN’s top 10 holdings account for 36.7% of its portfolio. This is lower than XIU’s 43.2% but higher than the S&P 500 index’s 33.3%. Therefore, ZCN’s concentration risk appears to be moderate.

ZCN

S&P 500

8.3% (RY)

6.6% (AAPL)

Top 10 Holdings

36.7%

43.2%

33.3%

Currency and geopolitical risks

Investors wishing to invest in Canadian equity funds need to keep in mind of geopolitical risks outside of Canada. Uncertainties surrounding the Trump administration’s policies can cause market turmoil. Since ZCN invests in Canadian equities, investors should also keep in mind of currency risks. A strengthening or weakening Canadian dollar can cause ZCN’s fund price volatility.

Investor Takeaway

ZCN offers exposure to Canadian equities with moderate concentration risk and a tilt toward cyclical sectors such as energy and materials. While this overexposure may improve performance during economic expansions, it also increases downside risk in economic contraction. In addition, ZCN’s underexposure to technology weakens its long-term performance prospect. Therefore, the fund should not be seen as a core holding, but a satellite position in one’s diversified portfolio.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: CNO Financial: Hidden Gem
data.description:
Investment Thesis

CNO Financial Group, Inc. (CNO) is a group of insurance companies that develops, markets, and administers health insurance, annuity, individual life insurance, and other financial services. They focus on serving middle-income pre-retiree and retired Americans. Their three primary brands are Bankers Life, Washington National, and Colonial Penn.

In the past five years, CNO has been quietly beating the S&P 500 Index (SP500) and its peers. The 5-year total return of CNO is 278%, while the total return of S&P 500 index is around 90%.

Their strategy of focusing on an underserved market with high-growth potential has been working.

I will share my analysis and outlook on CNO in this article.

2024 Performance

CNO has been growing their business nicely in the past couple of years. Their total insurance product margin increased from $936.5 M in 2022 to $1,040 M in 2024, and their net operating income grew from $360.4 M in 2022 to $429.3 M in 2024.

All three segments performed well, with the Annuity margin increasing from $227 M in 2022 to $274 M in 2024, Health margin increasing from $504 M in 2022 to $517 M in 2024, and Life margin increasing from $205 M to $249 M.

The 4Q 2024 annuity margin performed very well, growing from $50.7 M in 4Q 2023 to $55.0 M in 4Q 2024. Growth in the block was the main driver for the growth of fixed indexed annuities, but it was offset by the spread compression driven by increased surrenders of higher spread products. The elevated mortality resulted in a decrease in insurance liabilities and insurance policy benefits, and which led to a higher margin from other annuities ($3.5 M in 4Q 2024 against $1.5 M in 4Q 2023).

The Health segment also performed well in 4Q 2024, increasing the margin from $124 M in 4Q 2023 to $134 M in 4Q 2024. Their supplemental health products (e.g., specific disease, accident, and hospital indemnity products) grew substantially, which resulted in a higher margin. Long-term care performed very well, and the margin increased from $25.7 M in 4Q 2023 to $33.8 M in 4Q 2024. The favorable claim fluctuation pattern was the main driver of the growth.

In the Life segment, favorable mortality and growth in the block due to sales in the recent couple of quarters resulted in modest growth. The margin increased from $65.9 M in 4Q 2023 to $68.0 M in 4Q 2024. A decrease in marketing expense and growth in the block was the main contributor to this growth.

The total net investment income grew substantially as well from $280.8 M in 4Q 2023 to $325.4 M in 4Q 2024. The average book value of invested assets increased by 10%, which marks the 10th consecutive quarter of the book yield increase.

The earned yield improved to 5.16%, compared to 4.71% in the previous quarter and 4.49% in 4Q 2023.

Strong Capital and Liquidity

CNO has a solid capital structure and plenty of liquidity to maintain stable operation and support growth. The consolidated risk based capital ratio, which they target to stay above 375%, was 383% for 2024. Having a higher consolidated risk based capital ratio creates a cushion for the insurance company to cover their liabilities.

Market volatility and interest rate fluctuation impacted the consolidated risk-based capital ratio for CNO, but they successfully maintained their target ratio.

CNO also was able to increase their liquidity. Holding company liquidity, which they target to maintain above $150 M, increased from $167 M in 2022 to $372 M in 2024. CNO issued $700 M in senior notes 2Q 2024, of which $500 M was earmarked to repay the debt maturing in mid-2025. Excluding the $500 M debt that matures in the middle of this year, CNO was able to maintain the target debt to capital ratio (25-28%) in 2024 with 25.6%.

The strong capital structure and liquidity reassures investors that CNO will be able to maintain stable operations during times of uncertainty and be able to execute their growth plan in the future.

Future Outlook

I expect CNO to continue to improve their operation efficiency and revenue going forward. Management reinforced by belief when they provided positive guidance in the latest earnings call. The run rate operating return-on-equity is expected to improve by 150 basis points over the next three years (50 basis point improvement in 2025 to 10.5% of a run rate operating return-on-equity).

The operating return-on-equity improvement will be achieved through four different pillars: Products (accelerating brokerage and advisory services growth and introducing new products), Distribution (optimizing agent count and productivity, increasing synergy between fields and D2C, expanding worksite division geographic), Investment (optimizing capital structure, allocating assets strategically, and optimizing reinsurance), and Operational Efficiencies (Improving expense discipline, enhancing customer service, and modernizing technology platform).

Free cash flow of $200-250 M is expected for the holding company in 2025, and the risk based capital ratio is expected to be around 375%. The holding company liquidity will be above $150 M for 2025.

Risk

The stock and bond markets are experiencing unprecedented volatility caused by trade wars. The headlines from the Trump administration are causing the stock market to seesaw by more than 5% a day, and the retaliatory tariffs from other countries are adding more uncertainty to the stock market. Concerns over a recession and U.S. government debt is causing a U.S. treasury market sell-off, and the bond yields have been fluctuating.

Such volatile markets can cause the investment assets of CNO to swing wildly, and could cause loss of capital for CNO.

The risk of recession has been increasing due to the aforementioned trade war. During recessions, business activities slow, and the demand for insurance tends to go down, which will hurt CNO’s growth path. Therefore, investors should monitor the macroeconomic environment, and the development or escalation of the trade wars.

Conclusion

CNO has been quietly beating the market for the past several years. Given their solid underlying business and operating efficiency, I believe they will be able to continue their streak for the next several years.

Their strong capital structure and liquidity should also contribute positively to their growth plan and efficiency improvement plan.

Trade wars, market volatility, and a potential recession could negatively impact CNO’s growth trajectory. However, I believe CNO will be able to weather the challenges to sustain their long-term growth trajectory.

An investor who is looking for a solid company with a market beating potential for the next several years should consider CNO Financial Group.

data.title: Dana May Face Tariff Headwinds But Is Unbelievably Cheap
data.description:
Dana Incorporated (NYSE:DAN) is navigating a challenging market, positioning itself for the next upcycle with an improved operating model set up for stronger profitability. eFY25 is expected to be a trough year as the firm faces significant headwinds in e1h25 as trade across North America faces a trade war with 25% imposed tariffs on Mexico and Canada, potentially leading to a challenging automotive market in the near-term.

Despite the potential top-line challenges, adjusted EBITDA margins are expected to improve going forward, with a target of $300mm in annualized cost savings. Given these factors, I am recommending DAN shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $16/share at 4.72x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Dana Operations

Dana has faced significant headwinds as the trucking sector continued to experience a slower-than-expected selling cycle.

Despite the challenged top line, Dana has made significant improvements to operations that resulted in improved profitability, printing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 8% in Q4 ’24, up from 6.26% in the previous year’s quarter. Much of the performance improvement was the result of consolidating Dana’s production plants and other cost reduction measures, resulting in $100mm in cost savings in FY24.

Management is targeting $300mm in annual cost savings by 2026. The cost savings initiative includes downsizing corporate overhead, reducing complexity, and revising the firm’s EV commercial strategy. Accordingly, management will be curtailing capital spending on EVs to reflect the market demand.

One of management’s major initiatives is to divest Dana’s off-highway business in order to simplify the business and focus on light and commercial vehicle customers. This segment provides drive and motion systems for heavy-duty vehicles in the agriculture, materials handling, mining, construction, and forestry markets. The proceeds from the sale will be used to improve the balance sheet while simplifying the organization. Though this may be a quick fix for the firm, I believe that the deal will come at a time with domestic productivity coming more into focus, potentially leading to Dana not realizing the upswing in serviced markets.

From a broader perspective, management is anticipating a stabilized market for commercial vehicles towards the end of 2025 as a result of the 2026 emissions legislature change. The EPA issued a final rule on the matter on March 20, 2024, for multipollutant emissions standards for model years 2027 and later for light-duty and medium-duty vehicles. On March 14, 2025, the EPA issued an update to tailpipe emissions, with President Trump seeking to roll back on regulation. This can potentially extend out the transition to EV models for the industry, leaving internal combustion engines as the favorable solution.

Dana may face some indirect tariff risk in eFY25, as the imposed 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico remain in place. Though management indicated on the Q4 ’24 earnings call that additional costs can be passed through to customers, OEMs may slow down production as a result of the higher cost of materials. This in turn can be impacted by a decline in consumer discretionary spending as consumers are faced with higher costs of daily items, such as food and shelter. Heavy duty vehicles may also face significant headwinds as fleet operators are also sensitive to price increases. Higher costs may result in fleet operators either purchasing fewer trucks, pushing out purchasing, or turning to the used vehicle market in the instance of a pickup in activity.

Freight loads appear to be recovering in a significant way since the beginning of 2025 when compared to the previous two years. This could either be a signal to the market that demand is increasing, or truck loads are larger with fewer trucks on the road.

Dana Financial Position

Dana experienced a -6% top-line decline, driven by a -20% decline in off-highway sales in Q4 ’24. Management noted that pressure on sales was the result of lower OEM production of heavy vehicles. eFY25 is expected to be a challenging year for Dana as the market for vehicle sales slows down, paired with a decline in Dana’s EV program. Nonetheless, management guided stronger adjusted EBITDA for eFY25 as they remain focused on consolidating operations to improve profitability. Management is expecting annualized cost savings for eFY25 to sum to $175mm.

For Q1 ’25, I’m forecasting Dana to generate $2.23b in net revenue, with an adjusted EPS of $0.17/share.

Management’s cost-savings initiatives and potential divestiture of its off-highway segment is expected to lower the cost structure by $300mm by 2026. Management will utilize these savings to reduce the firm’s leverage back down to 1x EV/aEBITDA, a significant improvement when compared to its 2.39x leverage ratio in q4’24. Dana currently holds $495mm in cash & equivalent and $2.6b in debt on the balance sheet.

Risks Related to Dana

Bull Case

Dana is currently rightsizing operations by consolidating corporate operations, with the target of $300mm in annual cost savings. Despite the market headwinds, Dana may realize improved margins going forward, potentially leading to an uplift in adjusted EBITDA generation. Looking out to the long run, Dana may realize additional cost improvements if more automotive production is transitioned to the US, potentially reducing costs related to transportation.

Bear Case

The automotive market may face significant headwinds as trade tariffs reroute international trade, particularly between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico. The 25% imposed tariff may impact Dana indirectly through higher vehicle costs, potentially resulting in fewer fleet and vehicle sales.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Dana currently trades at 4.35x EV/aEBITDA, a significant discount to the firm’s midpoint trading valuation of 5x. This may provide significant upside potential, particularly when considering the recent market selloff at the beginning of April 2025.

Despite the less optimistic near-term outlook for growth, I believe Dana may realize value through its improved operating model. Given that the top-line decline is likely priced in, I believe shares may be driven by incremental cost savings going forward. With this in mind, I am rating DAN with a BUY rating with a price target of $16/share at 4.72x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Delta Air Lines: Hold For Now Amid Uncertainty, Long-Term Story Intact (Downgrade)
data.description:
Let’s be real—Delta Air Lines, Inc. (NYSE:DAL) has taken a hit. The stock’s down over 35% since the previous Buy recommendation at $62, and at this point I think it makes more sense to shift the stance to Hold. This isn’t about Delta suddenly becoming a bad business. It’s more about the combination of rising costs, murky macro conditions and a subtle—but important—shift in how management is talking about the future. Most notably, they pulled their full-year 2025 guidance, which doesn’t exactly scream confidence.

So here’s the situation: the long-term setup still has some legs. Delta is a well-run airline with a valuable loyalty program and solid international exposure. But near term? There’s too much uncertainty to pound the table on a fresh buy. The stock might look cheap, but I think the market is pricing in those risks more accurately now.

Is the Market Misreading Delta?

I don’t think the stock is screamingly mispriced right now. At ~$40 it’s trading around 10x forward earnings, which sounds low, but that multiple only matters if earnings hold up—and right now, that’s kind of up in the air.

The market isn’t ignoring the good stuff: loyalty revenue from Delta’s American Express (AXP) partnership hit $1.9 billion in Q1 alone, up 13% year-over-year. That part of the business throws off a ton of high-margin cash, and it’s honestly a bit of a secret weapon. But it’s not enough to offset rising costs across the board—labor maintenance, fuel, you name it. And because management isn’t offering forward guidance, investors are left guessing what the rest of 2025 is going to look like. That guessing game makes it tough to argue for a higher valuation multiple in the near term.

What Management is Actually Saying

There was a noticeable change in tone from Delta this quarter. They’ve typically been confident, even borderline optimistic, but that’s cooled a bit. Yes, revenue was up (2% year-over-year to $14.04 billion) and they stayed profitable. But operating income dipped to $569 million from $614 million last year. So the top line is fine, but margins are getting squeezed.

Salaries jumped over $290 million year-over-year. Contracted services, maintenance and landing fees all went up too. Those aren’t flukes—they’re part of the new baseline cost structure. Management mentioned they’re shifting toward “network flexibility” and “capacity discipline” which is corporate-speak for “we’re being cautious because we don’t love what we’re seeing.”

On the loyalty side, things are still humming. The SkyMiles program is pulling in cash and continues to be a standout both in terms of revenue recognition and deferred revenue build-up (now at $8.98 billion). If you want a steady recurring revenue stream inside an airline this is it.

Let’s Talk Valuation

So the numbers look something like this: Delta’s enterprise value is around $48 billion, trailing EBITDA is about $7.9 billion and free cash flow in Q1 was solid at $1.28 billion. The EV/EBITDA ratio is ~6x which isn’t demanding. Price-to-sales is about 0.45x. Compared to history and peers like UAL or LUV those are decent metrics.

But here’s the thing—cheap doesn’t always mean undervalued. Debt is still heavy at $15.1 billion with nearly $3 billion coming due in the next 12 months. They’ve got the liquidity to handle it ($3.7 billion in cash plus $3.1 billion in credit lines) but interest expense is eating into earnings—$179 million in Q1 alone. Until they clean up more of that debt or unless earnings rebound hard, I don’t see investors rewarding the stock with a higher multiple.

What the Headlines Are Saying

If you’ve skimmed the news lately, the general tone around Delta is neutral to slightly optimistic but nothing exuberant. They beat Q1 expectations, but the real story was them yanking full-year guidance. That move got more attention than the earnings beat did.

There’s also some macro noise: the tariff flip-flopping, oil price volatility and concerns about business travel softening. Delta’s international exposure was once a tailwind—now it’s more of a question mark. Even articles that try to spin the earnings as a net positive keep coming back to uncertainty around margins and forward visibility. In short, people are watching, but they’re not jumping in with both feet.

Risks You Can’t Ignore

Some of these risks are well known, but still worth calling out:

  • Labor costs are the big one. Salaries were up 7.7% YoY and that’s likely to continue as new union contracts kick in.

  • Fuel costs and refinery margins are swinging again. Delta runs its own refinery (Monroe) and while that can hedge fuel costs it’s also a complex volatile business.

  • Demand elasticity is starting to show. Domestic revenue fell 1% despite capacity growth. That’s the signal travelers are getting price-sensitive.

  • Debt overhang is manageable but not ideal. With $15B in total debt even modest rate increases or refinancing risks could stress cash flow.

  • No guidance from management just leaves too much open to interpretation. When expectations aren’t anchored, investor trust wobbles.

Scenarios: What Could Happen From Here

(Bear Case: $35): Fuel spikes, tariffs expand and demand drops further. Q2 guidance ends up too optimistic. Margins compress and investors start bracing for earnings downgrades. That takes us back to the $34–35 range we saw earlier this month.

(Base Case: $44–48): Delta sticks the landing on Q2. Premium and loyalty revenues hold up. Fuel stays low. There’s no blowout upside but no disaster either. The stock churns sideways in the mid-40s as investors wait for clarity.

(Bull Case: $55–60): Economic pressure eases. Corporate travel recovers. Delta reinstates guidance and posts stronger Q3 results. The market re-rates the stock and we’re back near the $60s—but that outcome needs a macro tailwind.

Bottom Line

Right now, Delta looks like it’s in a holding pattern. The business itself isn’t broken—far from it. But with cost pressure building and guidance off the table, it’s hard to know what to expect next. The market’s pulled back for a reason and while some of that looks like an overreaction, I don’t think we’ve hit the inflection point yet.

I’d stay on the sidelines for now. Watch how Q2 shakes out, especially if they bring back guidance or show signs of margin recovery. Until then, I’d say: don’t sell in a panic but don’t back up the truck either. Hold your position, keep your eye on the loyalty numbers and cost trends, and wait for clearer skies.

data.title: Equity Volatility Jumps The Most As Trump Tariffs Roil Markets
data.description:
Cross-Asset Volatility: Implied volatilities surged across asset classes last week as fears of recession jumped on the back of Trump’s tariff announcements. Equity volatility saw the biggest increase, with the VIX® index gaining 24 pts to a high of 45. Credit volatility jumped as spreads widened, with VIXIG almost doubling to a high of 70 bps vol. Rates vol increased (VIXTLT +24 pts wk/wk) as bonds rallied. Notably, inflation breakevens have fallen across the board post “Liberation Day” as negative growth fears outweigh any positive price impact from the tariffs. This comes partly as oil prices have collapsed, down over 11% last week. Despite the large spot move, oil vol remains the cheapest cross-asset vol, with WTI 1M implied vol trading just barely above its 10-year average vs. the VIX which is trading.

Equity Volatility: While SPX saw large drawdowns on both Thursday and Friday last week, the reaction in the vol market was markedly different. Thursday’s sell-off caused very little panic in the options market. SPX ATM vol jumped on the back of the increase in realized vol, but skew, convexity, and vol-of vol all barely increased (Exhibit 2). That all changed on Friday, as hedging demand picked up dramatically. SPX 1M skew (25- delta spread) gained 6.3 pts Friday (vs. 2.2 on Thursday) while vol-of-vol surged 28 pts (vs. unch’d on Thursday). Remarkably, SPX 1M implied-realized vol spread didn’t turn positive until Friday, in a sign that investors have consistently underpriced Trump’s tariff risk until now. Even Friday’s move paled in comparison to last August 5th , when VIX jumped to an intraday high of 65. While SPX closed down just 3% last August, VIX saw a similar increase (+15 pts) as this past Friday when SPX fell 6%. This is because skew, convexity, and vol-of-vol all saw much bigger increases (esp. convexity which gained twice as much).

Term Structure: SPX term structure is now the most inverted since the depth of the covid sell-off, with the 1Y-1M vol spread widening from -0.9% to -14.6%. Most of that is driven by the front-end, however. While SPX 1M vol trades near a 5 year-high, 1Y vol (at 22.9%) is still well below its 5-year high (at 31%) and even below its 2022 high (at 27%), suggesting

Correlation: As we highlighted at the beginning of the year, tariffs are macro risks, even as they may have disparate sector & stock impacts. S&P implied correlations surged higher last week, with the COR1M index rising from 19.5% to 45.8% post “Liberation Day”. Correlations increased across asset classes as well, with equity/rates, equity/credit, and equity/oil correlations all hitting 1-year highs (see pg 3).

Disclaimers:

• The information provided is for general education and information purposes only. No statement provided should be construed as a recommendation to buy or sell a security, future, financial instrument, investment fund, or other investment product (collectively, a “financial product”), or to provide investment advice.

• In particular, the inclusion of a security or other instrument within an index is not a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold that security or any other instrument, nor should it be considered investment advice.

• Past performance of an index or financial product is not indicative of future results.

• The views expressed herein are those of the author and do not necessarily reflect the views of Cboe Global Markets, Inc. or any of its affiliates.

• There are important risks associated with transacting in any of the Cboe Company products or any digital assets discussed here. Before engaging in any transactions in those products or digital assets, it is important for market participants to carefully review the disclosures and disclaimers contained at: https://www.cboe.com/us_disclaimers/

• These products and digital assets are complex and are suitable only for sophisticated market participants. In certain jurisdictions, including the United Kingdom, Cboe Digital products are only permitted for investment professionals, certified sophisticated investors, or high net worth corporations and associations.

• These products involve the risk of loss, which can be substantial and, depending on the type of product, can exceed the amount of money deposited in establishing the position.

• Market participants should put at risk only funds that they can afford to lose without affecting their lifestyle.

• © 2025 Cboe Exchange, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

data.title: Novavax: Regulatory Uncertainty Creates Risk And Opportunity For Beaten-Down Vaccine Developer
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Novavax (NASDAQ:NVAX) followed the announcement of a transformative collaboration with Sanofi (SNY) to develop COVID/influenza vaccines. The development, which included $500 million upfront and up to $700 million in milestone payments, allowed the struggling vaccine developer to ditch its “going concern” doubts in financial statements. Novavax was also developing its own combination vaccine. Last Fall, Novavax targeted Phase 3 initiation in Q4 2024. The hope is that COVID/influenza combination vaccines will replace seasonal influenza injections. Per Grand View Research, the global influenza vaccine market is valued at nearly $8 billion and is growing at a CAGR of ~7%.

However, Novavax is not alone in this venture. Other vaccine developers, like Pfizer (PFE) and Moderna (MRNA), are developing combination vaccines.

Although the Sanofi collaboration was a positive development, I’ve continued to side with caution on Novavax, rating it a Hold and noting its utility only in a barbell portfolio.

The stock has since fallen 56%.

The article that follows revisits Novavax in light of recent events.

Recent Kennedy Comments Impact Novavax Stock Performance

Novavax stock fell nearly 20% on Thursday after Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. cast doubts on Novavax’s COVID vaccination, Nuvaxovid.

Approved for use under the emergency use authorization, the company is in the process of securing full approval. However, the resignation of Dr. Peter Marks, who was a key vaccine regulator, and closer scrutiny of Novavax’s Biologics License Application (BLA) has delayed the FDA’s decision.

Secretary Kennedy, who reportedly spread vaccine misinformation in the past, stated in a recent interview that “single-antigen vaccines have never worked for respiratory illnesses.” Subsequently, under his leadership, the U.S. health authorities are “shifting priorities” towards multiple-antigen vaccines.

Scientific Assessment of Kennedy's Claims

Secretary Kennedy is right about one thing: Novavax’s COVID vaccination is a single-antigen vaccine. To be specific, it is a subunit vaccine that uses a recombinant spike protein from the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

However, he is likely wrong in stating it doesn’t work. Phase 3 data, published in the New England Journal of Medicine, demonstrated “strong evidence of high short-term vaccine efficacy for the prevention of Covid-19 (>90%) and for the prevention of moderate-to-severe disease (100%).” It was also found to be safe.

Moreover, many disagree with Secretary Kennedy’s claim that “single-antigen vaccines have never worked for respiratory illnesses.” Experts point to other COVID vaccines, RSV vaccines, and most influenza vaccines that target a single antigen. However, scientific evidence doesn’t matter as much now as it did before. So, we must assess how this change impacts Novavax’s prospects.

Impact on Sanofi Partnership

Fortunately, Novavax has already transitioned lead commercial responsibility of Nuvaxovid to Sanofi. This will begin with the 2025-2026 vaccination season. Relative to its competitors like Pfizer’s Comirnaty, Nuvaxovid never really caught on in the market, even when COVID was at its worst. Still, Sanofi found it to be of use, particularly in combination with its own influenza vaccines.

Unfortunately for Novavax, a $175 million milestone payment from Sanofi is contingent upon Nuvaxovid’s BLA approval. With this context, it appears that the market is assuming BLA approval is now off the table. Notably, Novavax’s market cap before Thursday’s news was $1.1 billion. So, it dropped by nearly this exact amount.

Current Corporate Strategy

Critically, this shouldn’t have major long-term implications for Novavax’s current corporate strategy. In the past, the company has unsuccessfully tried to independently develop and commercialize its own vaccines. Now, Novavax is prioritizing R&D activities focused on its technology platform and seeking partnerships for both early-stage and late-stage assets. Novavax is still advancing its own COVID/influenza vaccine and stand-alone seasonal influenza vaccine.

Combination Vaccine Development and Competitive Landscape

On that note, Novavax’s COVID/influenza combination vaccine began dosing participants in December 2024 and remains in Phase 3 trial. Initial data is expected by mid-2025.

Moderna is awaiting FDA approval for its mRNA-based vaccine, mRNA-1083. Pfizer and BioNTech (BNTX) experienced a setback in their Phase 3 program after revealing that its mRNA-based combination vaccine failed a key endpoint for the influenza B strain component. So, they could need a reformulation and additional clinical data to support regulatory approval.

Meanwhile, as part of the collaboration, Sanofi is advancing two combination vaccine candidates into Phase 1/2 clinical trials. These candidates combine Novavax’s COVID vaccine with Sanofi’s influenza vaccines. So, in a weird way, Novavax’s internally developed combination vaccine is also “competing” with Sanofi’s.

Novavax is also focused on earlier-stage assets like H5N1 avian pandemic influenza, RSV, shingles, and C. Diff. These are all in preclinical phases.

Earnings

In Q4, total revenues were $88.311 million. This is divided into product sales ($49.774 million) and licensing, royalties, and other ($38.537 million). This was a steep decline from $291.3 million in Q4 2023 due to lower product sales under Advance Purchase Agreements or APAs. Novavax has made steady efforts in cutting operating expenses. Total operating expenses decreased from $474.9 million in Q4 2023 to $219.4 million in Q4 2024. Still, its net loss in Q4 was $81 million.

Looking ahead to 2025, Novavax anticipated combined R&D and SG&A expenses to range from $475 million to $525 million. Adjusted licensing, royalties, and other revenue is expected somewhere between $300 million and $350 million. Notably, this includes the $175 million milestone payment upon the approval of its COVID vaccine BLA.

NVAX Stock Financial Health

As of December 31, cash and equivalents were $530.23 million. Marketable securities totaled $392.888 million. Total current assets were $1.129 billion, while total current liabilities were $1.154 billion. As a result, Novavax’s current ratio is just under 1, implying adequate near-term liquidity. The company does owe $169.684 million in long-term convertible notes.

Because Novavax had a number of one-off payments that have significantly impacted revenues in the recent quarters, I am going to use its expense/revenue estimates as a proxy for cash burn. Its original estimate implied a cash burn of $175 million (at the midpoint) in 2025. If we divide its cash and investments by this figure, it implies a cash runway spanning many years. However, this estimate is based on a number of assumptions (e.g., most of the revenue in 2025 is not recurring). So, there is a lot of nuance in these figures. I will say that if the $175 million milestone payment is thwarted, although this significantly shortens the cash runway, Novavax appears to have the financial flexibility to overcome it.

Conclusion

To wrap things up, Trump administration policies have caused a lot of uncertainty and volatility in financial markets. This is especially true of “vaccine stocks.” Novavax’s BLA delay is now an obvious example of the overreach. We must also not ignore the implications beyond just the Sanofi-related milestone payment. If U.S. health authorities change the rules for vaccine makers mid-game, this could drastically alter the outcomes of all of Novavax’s pursuits. This is an enormous risk. When it comes to rating NVAX, not much has changed fundamentally since my last evaluation. NVAX remains a speculative, high risk/high reward stock that could be of use in a barbell portfolio (maintain Hold). The company finally came to its senses and has altered its path for the good, despite its recent stock performance. Still, the financial (i.e. ongoing net losses, reliance on revenue from partnership milestone payments) and operational (i.e. regulatory, competitive) risks are massive and require careful consideration.

data.title: Danaos: +60% Discount To Book With A Reasonable Yield, Reiterate Buy
data.description:
Although Danaos Corporation (DAC) broke a six-quarter streak of missing EPS estimates, the Greek shipping company came in strong with a 41 bps beat in the final fiscal quarter of 2024. In my last article on DAC back in August 2024, right after Q2 results were announced, I noted that the stock price was far removed from the reality of strong fundamentals. Those fundamentals have held on, but the price seems to have further deteriorated between my last coverage and now.

Even discounting the recent dip-and-rise momentum of the broad market, DAC has mostly been trading sideways but in a very volatile manner. In the meantime, the yield has risen to above 4.7%, which includes the 5-cent increase in quarterly dividends over the past two payout periods.

That deserves a separate discussion now because the stock is literally available for cents on the dollar based on book value, and pays better than risk-free rates. We haven't seen these prices since early 2024.

Re-assessing the Fundamentals of DAC

From a revenue and profitability standpoint, Danaos hasn't done too badly despite the uncertainties that began in the fourth quarter of 2024. Quarterly operating revenues from drybulk show a near doubling, which includes the revenue boost from the additional 5.5 vessels (average) under utilization. Drybulk TCE revenues for the quarter grew almost four-fold on a YoY basis. Meanwhile, overall diluted adjusted EPS came in only marginally lower in Q4 2024 compared to Q4 2023, dropping from $6.99 to $6.93.

I see that overall fleet utilization dropped about 130 bps in Q4, and that's something to keep watching as tariffs continue to dominate the news cycle. It's likely we'll see fleet utilizations drop across all major shippers if and when the tariffs are implemented, but the worry for DAC is their China exposure. Per CEO Dr. John Coustas at the Q4 call:

We'll have to wait until after Chinese New Year to gauge the effect of the front-loading of exports that occurred in anticipation of tariffs and the demand pattern in the new trade environment.

Despite this low visibility into forward revenues, Dr. Coustas is positive on the future of the capesize segment where the company is investing in drybulk vessels. The new vessels delivered in Q2 and later bring its fleet up to 10 drybulk carriers, and despite the additions, utilization came in at 84.2% compared to 81.8% in the year-ago period.

As for their bread and butter segment, containerships, lower per-day TCE rates were mostly offset by 350+ additional operational days and a near-5-count increase in average vessels.

Although net income was down to $505 million in FY 2024 from $576 million in FY 2023, lower SBC, favorable working capital adjustments, and other non-cash items pushed CFO to near $622 million, up from $576 million in the year-ago quarter. That didn't translate to positive free cash flow due to the $659 spent on capex, but with drybulk showing promise even in light of the tariff threats, it's an area of investment that DAC can't afford to pass up.

That said, DAC continues to return to shareholders with the dividend increase, and the bulk of its share buybacks of $53 million was executed in Q4 - about $47.6 million of that, in fact. This is a significant point because it validates how cheap DAC has become in the past two quarters. I think management will keep a heavy foot on the buyback pedal while its stock languishes in the low single digit valuation multiples of 2.5x for its price to forward adjusted EPS and 0.35 for its price to forward book. I do see another board authorization coming up as DAC hits the $168.8 million mark on its current $200 million authorization.

What to Expect for DAC if a Recession Hits

The full-blown recession that many expect if the tariffs are fully implemented is only the beginning of DAC's problems. TCE rates are already showing signs of weakness, and higher rates the drybulk segment just aren't enough to fully offset any further declines. As Dr. Coustas pointed out, China's economy showing very slow recovery is definitely a major concern, and now that we have the makings of an all-out trade war between the U.S. and China, DAC's forward revenues have a big question mark hanging over them.

That being said, I'd still recommend DAC as a good place to park your money. Despite recession fears spooking the market and DAC operating in a segment that's highly prone to disruption by reduced demand for capacity, we need to keep one thing in mind: when the dust settles, shipping companies tend to benefit from new demand channels and better volumes across certain routes. The disruption in the interim, however, is unavoidable.

A further risk is that the threat of tariffs could force several nations targeted by the U.S. to export their wares to other markets in an attempt to offset the decline in U.S. demand. That might not be so simple, however, because the dollar is showing as much resilience as the U.S. economy is.

India seems to be the same. It scrambled to begin negotiations with the Trump administration, try to get the 27% reciprocal tariff down to 10%, per the same Reuters report cited above.

Regardless of how this plays out, the hit to the shipping industry is likely to be significant. Demand is likely to drop dramatically at first, and then gradually increase to fulfil the newly established trade flows. The containerized freight index has dropped significantly in the YTD period, but remains above pre-pandemic levels, so there's some room for play before bottom lines start getting hurt.

Within that larger context, DAC has a lot to offer as a kind of safe-haven investment. Its current assets of $671 million far outweigh the $169 million in short-term liabilities. As mentioned, capex came in very high in FY 2024, but I think it makes sense for DAC to grow its capacity in drybulk, where it can rapidly achieve the kind of scale needed to complement its containership dependency.

The current yield of over 4.7%, combined with the financial strength of the company, its continuing contributions to capesize orderbooks, and a massive $3.4 billion charter backlog will all serve well in the event of a massive trade disruption. The significant potential changes to trade flows and allocations from re-routing of goods will initially take a toll on operating expenses, so margin contraction is something investors will need to be prepared for.

Nevertheless, these headwinds are being met by strong fundamentals on DAC's part.

In the last six months alone, DAC's price to tangible book value multiple has deteriorated by over 23%. I know that DAC has historically traded well below book value, but this is now bordering on the ludicrous. Many investors also balk at Coustas not returning enough to shareholders via dividends, but balance that with their orderbook, huge backlog, aggressive buybacks, and excellent revenue visibility, and what you have is a very compelling risk to reward ratio that favors the upside. Therefore, I continue to rate DAC a Buy. If this valuation imbalance gets worse on the back of severe tariff-led disruptions or a recession, I may re-rate this a Strong Buy.
